narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 39—both above the typical averages for students in this dataset—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your upper-middle family income background and full-time enrollment plans. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 49% probability that you would fail it, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the school's risk of low bar passage rates. This prediction hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your third-year school decile ranking emerging as the single most influential element. That ranking, a 7th decile similar to your first-year performance, actually worked strongly in your favor, signaling to the model that you have the academic momentum to likely pass the bar. Yet, your family income bracket pushed moderately against you, as the model associates upper-middle income with slightly higher failure risks compared to other groups, perhaps due to patterns in how such backgrounds correlate with bar outcomes in the data. Your undergraduate GPA, despite being above average, also nudged the prediction toward failure risk, as the model saw it as not quite strong enough to fully offset other concerns. Overall, while your Year 3 decile pulled hardest toward success, the combined drag from income and GPA outweighed it, landing you in this borderline rejection zone—unlike many typical students whose profiles balance these factors more evenly for predicted passes. It's worth noting that 92.7% of students like you are full-time, so that didn't sway things much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant with your credentials; it's the model prioritizing bar passage stats above all. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push past 39 or bolstering your GPA narrative with explanations of upward trends, then reapply to schools with more holistic reviews. You could also target programs with strong bar prep support tailored to full-time students from similar backgrounds. Gathering letters highlighting your Year 3 improvement might help human reviewers see beyond the model's snapshot. With targeted steps, you're well-positioned to flip this outcome next time.
